vulnerability.title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
vulnerability.description: Check that Chrony only has time sources configured with the server directive.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive

triage_decision.reason: The fix only requires editing the chrony configuration file to remove non-server directives and restarting the chronyd service, which is a low‑impact change with no risk of locking out users.

review_feedback: Commenting out all non‑server directives can remove essential Chrony settings (e.g., driftfile, logdir, allow) and may prevent proper time synchronization or logging. The plan also lacks a backup and may unintentionally comment lines containing the word 'server' inside comments, risking misconfiguration.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will comment out all non‑server directives, which satisfies the finding, but it may remove necessary configuration items (e.g., driftfile, logdir, allow) that are required for proper Chrony operation. Adding a backup and targeting only the specific undesired directives would make the fix safer.
concerns: Commenting out essential directives could break time synchronization or logging.
No backup of the original configuration is performed before editing.
The sed command may unintentionally comment out lines that contain the word 'server' inside a comment or other context.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of /etc/chrony.conf before modification (e.g., cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak).
Explicitly list the directives to be removed (e.g., pool, allow, driftfile, makestep, etc.) instead of a generic regex.
Validate the configuration after editing with 'chronyd -p' or 'chronyd -Q' before restarting the service.
Consider using a configuration management tool (e.g., Ansible) to ensure idempotent changes.
